content.text: A robot navigating an indoor environment is surrounded by information that bounding boxes alone cannot convey. Knowing there is a detected object is useful; knowing that the floor ahead is navigable, the region to the left is a wall, the area directly ahead is an open door, and the shape in the corner is a person waiting — that is what enables truly intelligent, context-aware motion. Semantic segmentation assigns a class label to every pixel in the image, transforming raw camera data into a spatially dense understanding of the scene.

Semantic segmentation is the task of assigning a class label to every pixel in an image. Unlike object detection, which produces bounding boxes around discrete objects, segmentation produces a dense prediction map of shape H×W where each value is an integer class index. The result is a complete pixel-level parsing of the scene into categories such as floor, wall, ceiling, person, door, table, or obstacle.
Three related tasks form a hierarchy.

assigns a class per pixel but does not distinguish between different instances of the same class — all pixels belonging to "person" get the same label regardless of whether there are two people in the scene.
(Mask R-CNN, SOLOv2) generates a separate binary mask per detected object instance, giving each person a unique ID.

(Panoptic FPN, Mask2Former) unifies both: it assigns class labels to every pixel (including amorphous "stuff" like floor and sky) and also distinguishes individual countable "things" (people, cars, tables).

For robot navigation, the distinction between what is navigable and what is not is spatial and continuous. A bounding box around a table gives the robot a rectangular exclusion zone that may include perfectly navigable floor area beside the table's legs. A semantic mask assigns exactly the pixels occupied by the table as obstacle and leaves the adjacent floor pixels as free. This spatial precision translates directly to a higher-quality costmap and more efficient path planning.

Beyond navigation, semantic segmentation enables semantic scene understanding that supports higher-level robot behaviours: identifying a charging dock, recognising the entrance to a specific room type, detecting spilled liquids as traversal hazards, or distinguishing a hallway from an office. This richer scene representation is what separates a robot that avoids obstacles from one that understands its environment.
Pixel-precise obstacle boundaries: no false exclusion of navigable space beside objects.
Class-aware costmaps: floor pixels cost 0, wall pixels cost max, and person pixels apply a dynamic obstacle model.
Semantic maps: fusing segmentation output with LiDAR depth produces a semantically labelled 3D map usable for long-range planning.
Transfer from rich datasets: models trained on Cityscapes or ADE20K transfer well to robotic indoor/outdoor scenes with modest fine-tuning.
Compatible with TensorRT INT8: most FCN/DeepLab models quantise with less than 1% mIoU drop, enabling real-time edge deployment.
Higher compute than detection: dense prediction over all H×W pixels is more expensive than detecting a handful of boxes.
Requires per-pixel annotation: labelling datasets is labour-intensive compared to bounding-box annotation; tools like CVAT and Label Studio help.
Class imbalance: floor pixels vastly outnumber rare classes (fire extinguisher, step); weighted cross-entropy or focal loss is needed during training.
Boundary ambiguity: segmentation boundaries are soft at object edges; thin structures (chair legs, cables) are frequently misclassified.
Semantic segmentation alone has no depth: the mask tells you what is where in the image plane but not how far away it is — LiDAR or depth camera fusion is needed for 3D.

The Fully Convolutional Network (FCN) established the foundational architecture in 2015: replace the final fully connected layers of a classification CNN (VGG, ResNet) with 1×1 convolutions, then upsample via transposed convolutions to restore the original resolution. Skip connections from earlier layers improve boundary precision. FCN remains a strong, fast baseline for robotic use.

U-Net, originally designed for medical image segmentation, extends FCN with a symmetric encoder-decoder and skip connections at every resolution level. The decoder progressively recovers spatial detail lost in pooling, making U-Net excellent for applications requiring precise boundary localisation — such as navigable floor region delineation.

DeepLabv3+ uses an Atrous Spatial Pyramid Pooling (ASPP) module to capture multi-scale context without losing resolution. Atrous (dilated) convolutions insert gaps between kernel elements, effectively expanding the receptive field without additional parameters. ASPP applies multiple atrous convolutions in parallel at different rates and concatenates the results, enabling the model to recognise objects at widely varying scales — a critical property in outdoor environments where objects range from nearby kerbs to distant buildings.

Mask2Former (2022) introduces a universal segmentation architecture based on transformer cross-attention. A set of learnable object queries interact with multi-scale image features via masked cross-attention — each query attends only to its predicted object region. A single Mask2Former model achieves state-of-the-art results on semantic, instance, and panoptic segmentation benchmarks by simply changing the loss function during training.

attrs.alt: Semantic segmentation pipeline: RGB image through backbone and segmentation head produces label mask, feeding a semantic map for robot navigation. Comparison of semantic, instance, and panoptic segmentation types.

content.text: The pipeline flows left to right: RGB input through backbone feature extractor, segmentation decoder head, and dense label mask output. The semantic map translates class IDs into navigation costs. The inset compares semantic (class per pixel), instance (unique ID per object), and panoptic (stuff + things unified) variants.
Floor/obstacle/hazard delineation: when bounding boxes give insufficiently precise free-space boundaries for costmap generation.
Semantic mapping: to tag 3D point cloud regions with class labels for higher-level planning (e.g., "navigate to the kitchen").
Outdoor robots: Cityscapes-trained models parse roads, pavements, vegetation, and vehicles with high accuracy, enabling autonomous outdoor navigation.
Medical and surgical robots: segment tissue types, instruments, and anatomical structures in endoscopic imagery.
Precision agriculture: classify crop vs. weed vs. soil to drive targeted actuation.
The fastest path to a working segmentation model is to fine-tune a pre-trained DeepLabv3+ or SegFormer backbone from

on your robot's annotated dataset. For indoor navigation, fine-tune on a mix of ADE20K and your own labelled frames, remapping classes to your navigation taxonomy (navigable, obstacle, dynamic, forbidden). For ROS 2 deployment, wrap the inference in a node that subscribes to
computed from the projected floor vs. obstacle masks.

"""Fine-tune DeepLabv3+ (torchvision) on a custom robot navigation dataset.
Classes: 0=background, 1=floor, 2=wall, 3=obstacle, 4=person"""
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader, Dataset
from torchvision import transforms
from torchvision.models.segmentation import deeplabv3_resnet50, DeepLabV3_ResNet50_Weights
from torchvision.models.segmentation.deeplabv3 import DeepLabHead
from PIL import Image
import numpy as np
import os

NUM_CLASSES = 5  # background, floor, wall, obstacle, person

class RobotNavDataset(Dataset):
    def __init__(self, img_dir: str, mask_dir: str, img_transform=None, mask_transform=None):
        self.imgs = sorted(os.listdir(img_dir))
        self.masks = sorted(os.listdir(mask_dir))
        self.img_dir = img_dir
        self.mask_dir = mask_dir
        self.img_tf = img_transform
        self.mask_tf = mask_transform

    def __len__(self):
        return len(self.imgs)

    def __getitem__(self, idx):
        img = Image.open(os.path.join(self.img_dir, self.imgs[idx])).convert("RGB")
        mask = Image.open(os.path.join(self.mask_dir, self.masks[idx]))  # uint8 class map
        if self.img_tf:
            img = self.img_tf(img)
        if self.mask_tf:
            mask = self.mask_tf(mask)
        mask = torch.from_numpy(np.array(mask)).long()  # H x W, values 0-4
        return img, mask

def build_model(num_classes: int) -> nn.Module:
    model = deeplabv3_resnet50(weights=DeepLabV3_ResNet50_Weights.DEFAULT)
    # Replace classifier head for our number of classes
    model.classifier = DeepLabHead(2048, num_classes)
    return model

def train_one_epoch(model, loader, optimizer, criterion, device):
    model.train()
    total_loss = 0.0
    for imgs, masks in loader:
        imgs, masks = imgs.to(device), masks.to(device)
        optimizer.zero_grad()
        out = model(imgs)["out"]          # (B, C, H, W) logits
        loss = criterion(out, masks)
        loss.backward()
        optimizer.step()
        total_loss += loss.item()
    return total_loss / len(loader)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    device = torch.device("cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu")
    img_tf = transforms.Compose([
        transforms.Resize((480, 640)),
        transforms.ToTensor(),
        transforms.Normalize(mean=[0.485, 0.456, 0.406], std=[0.229, 0.224, 0.225]),
    ])
    dataset = RobotNavDataset("data/images", "data/masks", img_transform=img_tf)
    loader = DataLoader(dataset, batch_size=4, shuffle=True, num_workers=4)
    model = build_model(NUM_CLASSES).to(device)
    # Class-weighted loss to handle floor/background dominance
    weights = torch.tensor([0.2, 1.0, 1.0, 2.0, 3.0]).to(device)
    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss(weight=weights, ignore_index=255)
    optimizer = torch.optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=1e-4)
    for epoch in range(20):
        loss = train_one_epoch(model, loader, optimizer, criterion, device)
        print(f"Epoch {epoch+1:3d}  loss={loss:.4f}")
    torch.save(model.state_dict(), "deeplabv3_robot_nav.pth")

"""ROS 2 segmentation node: subscribes to /camera/image_raw,
runs DeepLabv3+, publishes coloured mask image and OccupancyGrid.
Floor (class 1) → free (0), everything else → occupied (100)."""
import rclpy
from rclpy.node import Node
from sensor_msgs.msg import Image
from nav_msgs.msg import OccupancyGrid
from cv_bridge import CvBridge
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F
from torchvision import transforms
import numpy as np

# Colour palette: 0=bg, 1=floor, 2=wall, 3=obstacle, 4=person
PALETTE = np.array([
    [20,  20,  20],   # background
    [70, 130, 180],   # floor — steel blue
    [220, 20,  60],   # wall — crimson
    [255, 165,  0],   # obstacle — orange
    [ 60, 179, 113],  # person — medium sea green
], dtype=np.uint8)

class SegmentationNode(Node):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__("segmentation_node")
        self.declare_parameter("model_path", "deeplabv3_robot_nav.pth")
        self.declare_parameter("num_classes", 5)
        self.bridge = CvBridge()
        self.device = torch.device("cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu")
        self._load_model()
        self.tf = transforms.Compose([
            transforms.ToTensor(),
            transforms.Normalize(mean=[0.485, 0.456, 0.406], std=[0.229, 0.224, 0.225]),
        ])
        self.sub = self.create_subscription(
            Image, "/camera/image_raw", self.image_cb, 10
        )
        self.mask_pub = self.create_publisher(Image, "/segmentation/mask_coloured", 10)
        self.grid_pub = self.create_publisher(OccupancyGrid, "/segmentation/occupancy", 10)

    def _load_model(self):
        from torchvision.models.segmentation import deeplabv3_resnet50
        from torchvision.models.segmentation.deeplabv3 import DeepLabHead
        n = self.get_parameter("num_classes").value
        path = self.get_parameter("model_path").value
        self.model = deeplabv3_resnet50(weights=None)
        self.model.classifier = DeepLabHead(2048, n)
        self.model.load_state_dict(torch.load(path, map_location=self.device))
        self.model.eval().to(self.device)
        self.get_logger().info(f"Model loaded from {path}")

    @torch.no_grad()
    def image_cb(self, msg: Image):
        bgr = self.bridge.imgmsg_to_cv2(msg, "bgr8")
        rgb = bgr[:, :, ::-1].copy()
        inp = self.tf(rgb).unsqueeze(0).to(self.device)
        logits = self.model(inp)["out"]          # (1, C, H, W)
        pred = logits.argmax(1).squeeze().cpu().numpy()  # (H, W) uint8
        # Coloured mask
        coloured = PALETTE[pred]                 # (H, W, 3)
        mask_msg = self.bridge.cv2_to_imgmsg(coloured, "rgb8")
        mask_msg.header = msg.header
        self.mask_pub.publish(mask_msg)
        # OccupancyGrid: floor=0 (free), all else=100 (occupied)
        occ = np.where(pred == 1, 0, 100).astype(np.int8).flatten().tolist()
        grid = OccupancyGrid()
        grid.header = msg.header
        grid.info.width = pred.shape[1]
        grid.info.height = pred.shape[0]
        grid.info.resolution = 0.01  # 1 cm per pixel (projection-dependent)
        grid.data = occ
        self.grid_pub.publish(grid)

def main():
    rclpy.init()
    rclpy.spin(SegmentationNode())
    rclpy.shutdown()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

"""Export a trained DeepLabv3+ to TensorRT INT8 for 2–4x faster inference
on NVIDIA Jetson Orin. Requires tensorrt and torch2trt packages."""
import torch
import numpy as np
from torch2trt import torch2trt
from torchvision.models.segmentation import deeplabv3_resnet50
from torchvision.models.segmentation.deeplabv3 import DeepLabHead

NUM_CLASSES = 5
INPUT_SIZE = (1, 3, 480, 640)  # batch=1

def load_model(path: str):
    model = deeplabv3_resnet50(weights=None)
    model.classifier = DeepLabHead(2048, NUM_CLASSES)
    model.load_state_dict(torch.load(path, map_location="cuda"))
    model.eval().cuda()
    return model

def export_trt_int8(model, calibration_dataloader, output_path: str):
    """Calibrate INT8 quantisation using a small representative dataset."""
    dummy_input = torch.randn(*INPUT_SIZE).cuda()

    model_trt = torch2trt(
        model,
        [dummy_input],
        fp16_mode=False,
        int8_mode=True,
        int8_calib_dataset=calibration_dataloader,
        max_batch_size=1,
    )
    torch.save(model_trt.state_dict(), output_path)
    print(f"TensorRT INT8 model saved to {output_path}")
    return model_trt

def benchmark(model, model_trt, n_runs: int = 100):
    import time
    x = torch.randn(*INPUT_SIZE).cuda()
    # Warmup
    for _ in range(10):
        _ = model(x)
        _ = model_trt(x)
    torch.cuda.synchronize()
    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    for _ in range(n_runs):
        model(x)
    torch.cuda.synchronize()
    t_pytorch = (time.perf_counter() - t0) / n_runs * 1000
    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    for _ in range(n_runs):
        model_trt(x)
    torch.cuda.synchronize()
    t_trt = (time.perf_counter() - t0) / n_runs * 1000
    print(f"PyTorch FP32: {t_pytorch:.1f} ms/frame")
    print(f"TensorRT INT8: {t_trt:.1f} ms/frame  ({t_pytorch/t_trt:.1f}x speedup}")

Waymo's research team published work on using semantic segmentation as an input feature for their pedestrian prediction and avoidance system. The segmentation model classifies road, pavement, building facade, vegetation, sky, person, and vehicle in each camera frame. The road and pavement labels feed directly into the drivable area mask used by the motion planner. Person labels trigger a class-specific prediction model that estimates pedestrian intent. The system operates on a Titan V GPU at 30 Hz for six cameras (one per side + forward/rear) — well within the vehicle's 100 ms planning latency budget.

For indoor mobile robots, the CMU LoCoBot research platform uses a real-time semantic segmentation model (DeepLabv3+, ResNet-50 backbone) to parse floor vs. obstacle regions and fuse the result with LiDAR occupancy in the Nav2 costmap. The segmentation-derived floor mask substantially reduces the number of false-positive obstacle cells compared to LiDAR-only perception in cluttered office environments where furniture legs are too thin for reliable LiDAR detection.
Using mIoU as the only evaluation metric: a high mIoU driven by dominant classes (floor, wall) hides poor performance on rare but safety-critical classes (step edges, wet floor signs).

Publishing the segmentation mask in the camera frame but using it as if it were a top-down floor map: segmentation is perspective; projecting it to a top-down occupancy map requires the camera's intrinsics and the floor plane geometry.
Not handling the ignore_index label in the loss: pixels labelled 255 (unlabelled) in many datasets must be masked out of the cross-entropy loss.
Training and deploying at different input resolutions without re-calibrating the ASPP atrous rates: the atrous rates are tuned for a specific input size.
Running inference on CPU for a model designed for GPU: FCN/DeepLab inference is 10–50x slower on CPU and will miss the navigation latency budget.
Use per-class IoU and frequency-weighted IoU alongside mIoU to catch models that ignore rare but critical classes.
Augment training data with random lighting, blur, and colour jitter to improve robustness to camera exposure variation in real deployments.
Apply test-time augmentation (horizontal flip, slight scale changes) for evaluation; it adds ~20% mIoU with no inference cost increase for batch processing.
Export to ONNX first, then TensorRT, to decouple model training from deployment optimisation and to validate numerical equivalence between frameworks.

Use temporal consistency post-processing (e.g., CRF or simple mask decay) when the segmentation output feeds a persistent costmap, to smooth flickering predictions that would cause jitter in planned paths.

Segmentation throughput scales with three factors: model size (number of parameters and FLOPs), input resolution, and hardware. FCN-ResNet50 at 640×480 achieves 18 ms on a Jetson Orin NX in FP16 mode — 55 FPS, well above the 10 Hz navigation control rate. DeepLabv3+ at the same resolution runs at 25 ms (40 FPS). Mask2Former with Swin-L backbone at the same resolution takes ~40 ms (25 FPS) but produces substantially higher mIoU on complex scenes. TensorRT INT8 calibration on 100–500 representative images reduces all of these by roughly 2x with under 1% mIoU degradation, making even Mask2Former feasible at 10 Hz.

Memory footprint is also a concern on embedded hardware: ResNet50-DeepLab consumes ~250 MB GPU memory for inference; Swin-L Mask2Former needs ~1.5 GB. Jetson Orin has 16 GB unified memory shared between CPU and GPU, so memory is rarely the bottleneck, but it becomes constraining if multiple models (detection + segmentation + SLAM) must co-reside.
Training framework for all major architectures
FCN, DeepLab, Mask2Former, SegFormer; COCO/ADE20K configs included
5000 fine-annotated images; 19 classes; standard AV benchmark
150 semantic categories; excellent for indoor robot fine-tuning
FP16 and INT8 with calibration; one-line conversion API
AI-assisted pre-labelling with SAM reduces annotation time 5–10x

Segmentation models are vulnerable to adversarial patch attacks — small printed patches on a wall or floor that cause the model to misclassify that region. More practically, distribution shift is a constant reliability concern: a model trained on a carpeted office will misclassify a reflective tile floor. Mitigation includes: collecting training data from the deployment environment, adding uncertainty estimation (Monte Carlo Dropout or conformal prediction), and building a safety layer that fuses segmentation with LiDAR-based occupancy so that disagreements trigger a conservative high-cost state rather than relying on one source alone.
Semantic segmentation assigns a class label to every pixel, giving robots dense, spatially precise scene understanding rather than coarse bounding boxes.
FCN, U-Net, and DeepLabv3+ are the workhorse architectures; Mask2Former provides state-of-the-art panoptic segmentation using transformer cross-attention.
Semantic, instance, and panoptic segmentation differ in whether they distinguish individual object instances and whether they label all background pixels.
Fine-tuning on domain data with class-weighted loss is essential — COCO or Cityscapes pre-training rarely covers all robot-specific classes accurately.
TensorRT INT8 calibration delivers 2–4x inference speedup with under 1% mIoU drop, enabling real-time deployment on Jetson Orin.
Segmentation output must be fused with a depth source (LiDAR or stereo) to produce a 3D semantic costmap useful for path planning.
Never rely solely on segmentation for safety; combine with geometry-based occupancy to maintain safety under distribution shift.
